observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to review, verify, and analyze the
book reconciling items in the BRS, and the staled checks that have been outstanding for
several years. Exhaust all possible means to locate the concerned payees, determine
whether these are still valid claims and that the concerned payees are still interested to
claim their checks, otherwise, consider the reversion to the Unappropriated Surplus of
the General Fund, especially those that have been outstanding for more than two years.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) prepare and submit to the Audit Team the lacking
BRS for two bank accounts under the 20% DF, and henceforth ensure that BRS
are regularly prepared for all bank accounts and duly submitted to the Audit
Team within the prescribed period; and (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting
journal entries to account for the valid book reconciling items, the errors noted
in recording and the reversion of stale checks, for fair and accurate presentation
of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements as at year-end; and
b. Acting Municipal Treasurer: (i) send written notices to the payees one month
before the check becomes stale; (ii) for stale checks already issued to the payees,
immediately inform them to return the same to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office,
for cancellation and issuance of the necessary check replacements thereof; (iii)
for unclaimed stale checks still in her possession, immediately cancel the same and
furnish the Municipal Accountant with the related original DVs and supporting
documents, to facilitate adjustment in the books; and (iv) henceforth ensure that
all checks issued are properly monitored, to avoid the accumulation of stale
checks.

In view thereof, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the
grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, to forestall the accumulation of
unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the
writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased, and those
separated from the service whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2016-005; (c) require the concerned AOs and employees to immediately settle their
outstanding cash accountabilities, otherwise, consider withholding the respective
salaries to enforce settlement thereof; and (d) intensify collection efforts by taking
advantage of electronic means, such as emails, to demand settlement from AOs with
outstanding balances, who are no longer connected with the LGU, but whose
whereabouts are still known.
Likewise, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant:
(a) verify and reconcile the noted discrepancies between the recorded Receivable
balances in the RUCA and the Statement of Financial Position, and make the necessary
adjustments thereof; (b) regularly send Demand Letters to AOs with unsettled cash
advances, particularly those that have already been outstanding for several years; and
(c) verify and analyze the validity of the over-liquidation and the nature of the negative
balances, and make the necessary adjustments therein, for the fair and accurate
presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the Inventory Committee to: (i) conduct a complete physical count on all
Inventories and PPEs of the Municipality, and henceforth ensure that these are
conducted semestrally for Inventories and annually for all PPEs; and (ii) prepare and
submit to the Audit Team, the corresponding RPCIs, RPCPPEs, and RPCLRN, copy
furnished the Accounting Office, to facilitate reconciliation with their records, and
thereafter ensure that RPCIs are submitted regularly, not later than July 31 and
January 31 of each year, and not later than January 31 of the ensuing year for RPCPPEs
and RPCLRN.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and GSO to maintain a complete and updated perpetual
inventory records, to facilitate periodic reconciliation of PPE accounts so that
discrepancies, if any, are immediately detected and corrected; and
b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) provide depreciation on all depreciable assets;
(ii) reclassify all the completed projects under the CIP asset accounts to its
respective PPE accounts; and (iii) verify and reconcile the P10,205,129.05
discrepancies between the reported PPE balances in the Statement of Financial
Position and the Schedule of PPEs, and make the necessary adjustments, thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, together with the
Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer:
(a) continue to implement cost-effective measures, and prudence in handling government
funds, pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445, to maintain its ability to meet current
obligations; and (b) ensure that funds under the 20% Development Fund, Trust Fund
and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF), among
others, are readily available for its intended purpose, in accordance with Section 305(e)
of RA No. 7160.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor support the tax
campaigns of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to increase the tax collection efficiency
of the LGU, and the Acting Municipal Treasurer to develop a more intensive collection
strategies and apply all available remedies provided by RA No. 7160, to collect the
delinquent taxes.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Acting Municipal Treasurer to prioritize the updating of the RPTARs and provide
the Municipal Accountant with a certified list of taxpayers, showing the amounts
due and collectible for the year, to facilitate the setting up of the correct RPT and
SET Receivable accounts and the corresponding Deferred RPT/SET accounts at
the beginning of each year; and
b. Municipal Accountant to set-up regularly the RPT and SET Receivables at the
beginning of each year, based on the submitted certified list of taxpayers and
amounts due and collectible for the year, prepared by the Acting Municipal
Treasurer, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected account balances in
the Financial Statements.

In view thereof, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) reconcile the noted discrepancies between the
reported balances of the Due from LGUs account in the Statement of Financial Position
and the Status of Fund Transfer to Other Government/IAs, and make the necessary
adjustments thereof; and (ii) refrain from granting additional fund transfers to other
LGUs until all previous accountabilities have been settled/liquidated and duly accounted
for in the books of accounts, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
Barangay and SK officials to immediately submit the liquidation/settlement documents
for all funds transferred by the LGU, and henceforth ensure that all funds transferred
are promptly liquidated/settled after the completion of the specific purpose of said
Funds.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify and reconcile the balances of the Due from and Due
to Other Funds account in the respective books, and prepare the necessary adjusting
journal entries to present the correct account balances in the Statement of Financial
Position; and (b) submit to the Audit Team, the detailed supporting schedules for the
said accounts and strictly monitor the reciprocal accounts by conducting regular
reconciliation thereof, to eliminate future discrepancies, if any.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify the validity of the corresponding
payable accounts, and if found to be valid, immediately notify the concerned creditors
of their outstanding claims. If found to be without valid claim, proper reversion should
be made to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund, particularly those aged
more than two years; (b) exhaust all means to provide all the necessary details/nature of
payables totaling P0.972 million, in the supporting schedules; and (c) prepare the
necessary adjusting journal entries for the reclassification of the amounts due to officers
and employees, and various individuals totaling P0.715 million, to their respective
Payable accounts, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the
Financial Statements.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require the: Municipal Accountant to: (a) cause the prompt and regular
remittance of the amounts due to the concerned LGUs, within the prescribed period of
remittance, pursuant to pertinent laws, rules and regulations, to augment funds and
provide basic services to their respective constituents; and (b) exhaust all possible means
to identify the nature, details, and the respective payees of the outstanding Due to LGUs
account balances totaling P0.641 million, for accurate reporting and proper monitoring
of accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, together with the
Human Resource Management Officer: (i) prepare a staff pattern plan/manpower
requirement for each unit/department, pursuant to Section 76 of RA No. 7160, to better
assess the LGU’s existing organizational structure; (ii) prioritize the hiring of regular
employees for the 32 vacant plantilla positions; and (iii) evaluate the need for hiring job
orders, casuals and contractual employees, based on the manpower requirements of the
LGU, as identified in the staff pattern plan, to ensure the reasonableness of hiring job
orders, casuals and contractual employees, and avoid unnecessary utilization of funds
through duplication of duties, overlapping of functions and/or work redundancy.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting
documents, and henceforth ensure that all payments to job orders, casuals and
contractual employees and consultants are properly supported with complete
documentation, pursuant to Section 4.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001, before
processing claims for payment; and (ii) closely monitor the refund of the noted
overpayments;
b. HRMO to: (i) ensure that work schedules of the non-permanent employees are
duly stipulated in the Job Order contracts/Contracts of Service, and/or services
rendered beyond the regular working days/hours, such as on weekends and
holidays, are duly supported with authority to render services thereof; and (ii) see
to it that DTRs are carefully verified and monitored, before the preparation of the
payrolls, to avoid any overpayments thereof; and
c. Acting Municipal Treasurer to see to it that payments to job orders, casuals and
contractual employees are received by the payees and/or their duly authorized
representatives.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor, together with the Municipal Development Council: (a) ensure the optimal
utilization of the 20% DF to spur development, provide jobs to constituents, and achieve
the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU, so
that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits derived from said
development projects; (b) ensure that all target PPAs under the 20% DF are duly
supported with cash to guarantee the implementation thereof; (c) see to it that all PPAs
included in the AIP are procurement-and-implementation-ready, to avoid any delays in
the project implementation; and (d) consider hiring additional Engineers and Draftsmen
in the Engineering Office to facilitate the efficient and effective implementation of the
priority PPAs.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require/instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents, for verification, and henceforth ensure that all claims are
duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents required under
COA Circular No. 2012-001, before processing for payment; and (ii) prepare the
necessary adjusting journal entries for the errors noted in recording, for fair and
accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements;
b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and BAC Secretariat to ensure that: (i) all
contact agreements/POs, together with the related supporting documents, are
submitted to the Audit Team, within five days after perfection; and (ii) NOAs,
contracts and NTPs are posted in the PhilGEPS website, within three and 15
calendar days from the issuance of NOA and NTP, respectively; and
c. OIC – Municipal Engineer to fast track the preparation of all project
requirements, such as POWs and Detailed Quantities and Cost Estimates, among
others, to facilitate the bidding procedures and the eventual implementation of
the priority PPAs under the 20% DF.

In view of the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor require/instruct the:
a. LDRRM Officer to ensure that all priority PPAs included in the LDRRMFIP are
duly covered with appropriation, and ensure that these PPAs are reviewed
regularly and updated annually, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002;
b. Municipal Accountant to: (i) transfer the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF
for CY 2024 to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liability – DRRM account
in the Trust Fund books; (ii) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents for verification, and henceforth ensure that claims are
duly supported with complete and proper documentation, to establish the
validity, regularity and propriety of the disbursements, before processing claims
for payment; (iii) make the necessary revisions and/or adjustments on the
submitted CY 2024 Report on Sources and Utilizations of the DRRMF; and
(iv) provide proper disclosure of the details of the unexpended LDRRMF
balances in the Notes to Financial Statements;
c. BAC to see to it that all procurements are made strictly in accordance with the
provisions of the updated 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
d. Acting Municipal Treasurer to ensure that payments are duly received by the
payees and/or their duly authorized representatives and that printed names of
the claimants are duly indicated in the DVs for proper identification.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD
Focal Point System: (a) ensure the prompt and optimal utilization of the GAD Funds, in
accordance with the provisions of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as
amended by JMC No. 2016-01, to fully achieve the objective of providing equal
development opportunities for men and women, through gender responsive PPAs; and
(b) see to it that expenditures charged against the GAD Funds are related and/or
strictly according with the approved project/program proposals, as endorsed by the DILG.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
GAD Focal Person and the Municipal Accountant to verify and reconcile the noted
discrepancy in the reported expenditures in the GAD AR and the GAD Utilization
Report, and make the necessary adjustments in their respective records, for a reliable
and accurate reporting.

In view of this, we recommended for the settlement of audit suspensions, and disallowances
and charges within 90 days and six months from receipt of the Notices of Suspension,
Disallowances, and Charges, respectively, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on
Settlement of Accounts. The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements
of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the OIC – Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer: (a) continue to implement cost-effective measures, and prudence in handling government funds, pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445, to maintain its ability to meet current obligations; and (b) ensure that trust receipts, and mandatory obligations under the 20% Development Fund and the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF), among others, are used solely for its intended purpose, in accordance with Sections 4(3) of PD No. 1445 and 305(e) of RA No. 7160.

BAC and the TWG to immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant all the lacking Bidding Documents and other related documents pertaining to the award of the contracts and submit a reasonable explanation on the noted deficiencies and inconsistencies in the award of the contracts;   


Municipal Accountant to:      (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment, as required under Sections 9.1, 9.1.1, and 9.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and     (ii) prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to account for the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; and

Municipal Engineer to:          (i) ensure that the Approved Budget for the Contracts are computed in accordance with the DPWH Department Order No. 197; and                           (ii) closely monitor the projects to ensure that these are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications and completed within the contract duration, otherwise, impose liquidated damages thereon.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System: (a) ensure that the GPB is prepared and submitted to the PPDO for review and subsequently to the DILG Provincial Office for its endorsement, within the prescribed timelines, to avoid delays in the project implementation; and (b) cause the optimal and judicious utilization of the GAD Funds, pursuant to the provisions of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by JMC No. 2016-01, to achieve the objective of providing equal development opportunities for men and women, through gender responsive PPAs.
observations.reason:
The CY 2021 GAD Plan and Budget is still not submitted to the PPDO and the DILG within the prescribed timelines. The GAD Focal Person attributed the late preparation of the GPB to the surge of COVID-19 cases in the Municipality causing lockdowns in affected Barangays.

Moreover, the utilization of the GAD Funds still registered a low financial delivery rate of only 17.01 percent due to the cancellation of several trainings and workshops due to the COVID-19 Pandemic, prohibiting face to face interactions. 

Also, some of the projects have already been implemented charged against a different Fund.

As such, this is reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (i) cause the return/refund of the remaining funds from NGAs and BGCM totaling P1,362,402.31; (ii) ensure strict compliance with the pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization, and implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs; and (iii) see to it that disbursements exceeding the P15,000.00 threshold are paid thru checks directly to the suppliers, pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents, for verification, and henceforth, ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment;
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that all procurements for COVID-19 related PPAs are made strictly in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, particularly in the award of contracts, to ensure that these are awarded to eligible bidders/suppliers; and
OIC - Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the disbursement vouchers are duly signed by the authorized Municipal officials before the release of payments thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer exercise prudence in handling government funds, pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445 to maintain its ability to meet current obligations, and ensure that the trust funds are used solely for its intended purpose, pursuant to Sections 4(3) of PD No. 1445 and 305(e) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to: (i) regularly prepare and submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts maintained by the Municipality; (ii) prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to account for the valid book reconciling items and stale checks, for fair and accurate presentation of the Cash in Bank – LCCA and the related Payable/Government Equity accounts in the Statement of Financial Position; and (iii) make representations with the concerned banks for the issuance of the bank statements for the timely preparation of the BRS; and
Municipal Treasurer: (i) to send written notices to the payees one month before the check becomes stale; (ii) for stale checks already issued to the payees, to immediately inform them to return the same to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, for the cancellation and issuance of the necessary check replacements thereof;  (iii) for unclaimed stale checks still in his possession, to immediately cancel the same and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the related original Disbursement Vouchers and supporting documents, to facilitate adjustment in the books; and (iv) henceforth ensure that all checks issued are properly monitored, to avoid the accumulation of stale checks.

In view thereof, we reiterated that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased, and those separated from the service whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; (c) consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers still connected with the LGU, to enforce full settlement thereof; and (d) intensify collection efforts by taking advantage of electronic means, such as emails and/or social media platforms to demand settlement from AOs with outstanding balances, who are no longer connected with the LGU, but whose whereabouts are still known.
Likewise, we reiterated that the Municipal Accountant: (a) regularly issue Demand Letters to AOs with unsettled cash advances, particularly those that have already been outstanding for several years; and (b) see to it that previous cash accountabilities of officers and employees are fully settled/liquidated and duly accounted for in the books of accounts before additional cash advances are granted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the newly constituted Inventory Committee to: (i) promptly and regularly conduct a complete physical count, semestrally for Inventories and annually for all PPEs of the Municipality; and              (ii) prepare and submit the corresponding RPCIs and RPCPPEs thereon. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Concerned departments/units to immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant the necessary liquidation reports and/or distribution lists of the related Inventory items that had already been distributed, for proper recording in the books, and henceforth ensure that such documents are promptly submitted to the Accounting unit as soon as the items had been issued to the intended recipients; and
Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the noted errors in recording, for fair and correct presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) verify the validity and existence of the payable accounts, and determine whether these are still valid and enforceable; (b) notify the concerned payees of their outstanding claims, if found to be without valid claim, proper reversion should be made to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund; and (c) prepare the necessary adjustments therein, if necessary, for fair and accurate presentation of the Payable accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to cause for the prompt and regular remittance of the amounts due to the concerned LGUs within the prescribed period of remittance, pursuant to pertinent laws, rules and regulations, to augment funds and provide basic services to their respective constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Planning and Development Council and the Municipal Engineer, ensure the optimal utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund to spur development, provide jobs to constituents and to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU, so that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits therefrom.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to analyze and verify the noted discrepancies and/or inconsistencies in their respective records; make the necessary adjustments therein; and henceforth ensure that both records are kept updated and reconciled at all times, for accurate and reliable reporting;
Municipal Budget Officer to see to it that all expenditures are charged against their specific appropriations. Changes and/or amendments in the approved PPAs, as indicated in the Annual Investment Plans (AIPs) and embodied in the Annual Budget, if any, shall be covered by an Appropriation Ordinance duly approved by the Local Sanggunian; and
Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking supporting documents, and henceforth, ensure the completeness and regularity of the supporting documents before processing claims for payment, to establish the regularity, validity and propriety of the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System: (a) ensure that the GPB is prepared and submitted to the PPDO for review and subsequently to the DILG Provincial Office for its endorsement, within the prescribed timelines, to avoid delays in the project implementation; and (b) cause the optimal and judicious utilization of the GAD Funds, pursuant to the provisions of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by JMC No. 2016-01, to achieve the objective of providing equal development opportunities for men and women, through gender responsive PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization and reporting of the status of funds and the implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs; and (b) see to it that changes and/or amendments in the Annual Budgets to accommodate COVID-19 related PPAs are covered with Appropriation Ordinance, duly approved by the Sangguniang Bayan, to ensure that all charges made thereto are expressly authorized by law, rules and regulations. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

BAC to ensure that all procurements for COVID-19 related PPAs are included in the supplemental APPs and revised AIPs approved by the local Sanggunian, and see to it that all documentary requirements are complied with before the award of the contracts, pursuant to the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; 

Municipal Accountant to:         (i) make the necessary adjustment to effect the receipt of BGCM funds, and the remaining balance to the affected accounts, and ensure that receipt of funds with the same nature are taken up in the books of accounts accordingly; (ii) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents, to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment; and (iii) ensure that purchases of relief goods for distribution are recorded under its proper inventory accounts, appropriate expense accounts shall be recognized only upon its issuance; and

Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile, analyze and verify the noted discrepancies and/or inconsistencies in their respective records; make the necessary adjustments therein; and henceforth ensure that both records are kept updated and reconciled at all times, for accurate and reliable reporting.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the optimal and judicious utilization of the GAD Fund pursuant to Section 5.b.iv of the PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01, to achieve the objective of providing equal development opportunities to men and women through gender responsive PPAs.
observations.reason:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization and reporting of the status of funds and the implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs; and (b) see to it that changes and/or amendments in the Annual Budgets to accommodate COVID-19 related PPAs are covered with Appropriation Ordinance, duly approved by the Sangguniang Bayan, to ensure that all charges made thereto are expressly authorized by law, rules and regulations. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
BAC to ensure that all procurements for COVID-19 related PPAs are included in the supplemental APPs and revised AIPs approved by the local Sanggunian, and see to it that all documentary requirements are complied with before the award of the contracts, pursuant to the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
Municipal Accountant to: (i) make the necessary adjustment to effect the receipt of BGCM funds, and the remaining balance to the affected accounts, and ensure that receipt of funds with the same nature are taken up in the books of accounts accordingly; (ii) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents, to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment; and (iii) ensure that purchases of relief goods for distribution are recorded under its proper inventory accounts, appropriate expense accounts shall be recognized only upon its issuance; and
Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile, analyze and verify the noted discrepancies and/or inconsistencies in their respective records; make the necessary adjustments therein; and henceforth ensure that both records are kept updated and reconciled at all times, for accurate and reliable reporting.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer exercise prudence in handling government funds, pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445 to maintain its ability to meet current obligations, and ensure that the trust funds are used solely for its intended purpose, pursuant to Sections 4(3) of PD No. 1445 and 305(e) of RA No. 7160.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 94-013 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers to implementing agencies, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased and those separated from the service whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers still connected with the LGU to enforce settlement thereof.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) refrain from granting additional cash advances to employees, and fund transfers to other LGUs with outstanding balances until all previous accountabilities have been settled/liquidated and accounted for in the books of accounts, to forestall accumulation of unsettled balances; (ii) regularly issue Demand Letters to AOs with unliquidated/unsettled cash advances, particularly those that have been outstanding for several years already; (iii) ensure that all claims are supported with complete, and proper documentation duly signed by the authorized officials before processing for payment; and (iv) prepare the necessary JEVs for the adjustments on the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 94-013 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances and fund transfers to implementing agencies, to forestall the accumulation of unsettled balances; (b) make a formal request with the Commission on Audit for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of those who are already deceased and those separated from the service whose whereabouts are unknown, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (c) consider withholding the salary of those employees and officers still connected with the LGU to enforce settlement thereof.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) refrain from granting additional cash advances to employees, and fund transfers to other LGUs with outstanding balances until all previous accountabilities have been settled/liquidated and accounted for in the books of accounts, to forestall accumulation of unsettled balances; (ii) regularly issue Demand Letters to AOs with unliquidated/unsettled cash advances, particularly those that have been outstanding for several years already; (iii) ensure that all claims are supported with complete, and proper documentation duly signed by the authorized officials before processing for payment; and (iv) prepare the necessary JEVs for the adjustments on the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor constitute the Inventory Committee, who shall: (i) promptly and regularly conduct the annual physical count on all Inventories and PPE of the Municipality; and (ii) prepare and submit the corresponding RPCIs and RPCPPEs therein.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Concerned departments/units to immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant the necessary liquidation reports and/or distribution lists of the related Inventory items that had already been distributed, for proper recording in the books, and henceforth ensure that such documents are promptly submitted to the Accounting unit as soon as the items had been issued to the intended recipients; and
Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the adjustments on the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the validity of the unliquidated obligations or Accounts Payable, aged two years or more, and cause the reversion of these accounts to the Unappropriated Surplus, if warranted.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to cause for the prompt and regular remittance of the amounts due to the concerned LGUs within the prescribed period of remittance, pursuant to pertinent laws, rules and regulations, to augment funds and provide basic services to their respective constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to ensure that all procurement activities undertaken by the Municipality are strictly in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, particularly during the bid evaluation and post-qualification phase, for proper evaluation and assessment of the eligibility as to legal, technical and financial capacities of the prospective bidders. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
BAC and the TWG to immediately submit to the Municipal Accountant all the lacking Bidding Documents and other related documents pertaining to the award of the contracts and submit a reasonable explanation on the noted deficiencies and inconsistencies in the award of the contracts;
Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking supporting documents to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents before processing for payment, as required under Sections 9.1, 9.1.1, and 9.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (ii) prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to account for the noted errors in recording, for fair and accurate presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; and
Municipal Engineer to: (i) ensure that the Approved Budget for the Contracts are computed in accordance with the DPWH Department Order No. 197; and              (ii) closely monitor the projects to ensure that these are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications and completed within the contract duration, otherwise, impose liquidated damages thereon.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to: (a) submit to the Municipal Accountant the revised PERT/CPM and/or S-Curve/Bar chart showing the proposed/actual accomplishment on December 26, 2017 (original expiration date), as basis for the determination of liquidated damages; (b) provide a plausible explanation and/or justification on the ABC and contract costs exceeding the COA Cost Estimate; and (c) ensure strict compliance with the DPWH Department Order No. 197 and COA Resolution No. 2015-014 in preparing the ABC.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, upon recommendation of the Municipal Engineer, compute for the appropriate liquidated damages and impose the same to the concerned contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
LDRRM Officer to prepare an updated LDRRMFIP, with complete details of the specifically identified programs and projects for disaster prevention, mitigation, and preparedness activities, including target PPAs for the all the unexpended balances under the Special Trust Fund, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002;
Municipal Accountant to: (i) transfer the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE to a Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund books, pursuant to Section 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002, and ensure that funds are available for disaster risk reduction and management activities; and (ii) provide disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements regarding the status of the unexpended LDRRMF balances, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002, for transparency and monitoring purposes; and
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their respective records. Discrepancies that may arise, if any, should be investigated accordingly for accurate and reliable reporting.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to analyze and verify the noted discrepancies and/or inconsistencies in their respective records; make the necessary adjustments therein; and henceforth ensure that both records are kept updated and reconciled at all times, for accurate and reliable reporting; and
Municipal Budget Officer to: (i) strictly monitor and regularly update the RAAO to ensure that expenditures do not exceed the available appropriations; and (ii) see to it that all expenditures are charged against their specific appropriations. Changes and/or amendments in the approved PPAs, as indicated in the Annual Investment Plans (AIPs) and embodied in the Annual Budget, if any, shall be covered by an Appropriation Ordinance duly approved by the Local Sanggunian.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the GAD Focal Point System: (a) ensure that the GPB is prepared and submitted to the PPDO for review and subsequently to the DILG Provincial Office for its endorsement, within the prescribed timelines, to avoid delays in the project implementation; and (b) cause the optimal and judicious utilization of the GAD Funds, pursuant to the provisions of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by JMC No. 2016-01, to achieve the objective of providing equal development opportunities for men and women, through gender responsive PPAs.

With these, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the OIC-Municipal Budget Officer, appropriate one percent of the share from IRA for priority programs, projects and activities for the Senior Citizen and PWDs, pursuant to RA Nos. 9994 and 9442, respectively.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspensions and disallowances within 90 days and six months from receipt of the Notices of Suspension and Disallowances, respectively, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts. The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: 

Acting Municipal Treasurer to cause for the immediate submission of the eight (8) DVs totaling P3,269,949.23 to the Municipal Accountant for proper recording in the books of accounts and require justification for his persistent failure to comply despite previous years’ audit recommendation; and 

Municipal Accountant to (i) draw JEV to record the checkbook requisition fee amounting to P500.00; (ii) follow up with the concerned servicing bank regarding the nature of the debit memos totaling P22,630.49 for the proper recording in the books of accounts; and (iii) prepare and submit the BRS of the two (2) depository accounts to reflect the correct Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
Reconciling items totaling P3,269,949.23 for the eight unsubmitted DVs had been adjusted in the books in CY 2020.

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant already recorded in the books, the checkbook requisition fee amounting to P500.00 and the debit memos totaling P22,630.49.
observations.reason:
The eight DVs totaling P3,269,949.23, although already recorded in the books, have not yet been submitted to the Audit Team, thus, could not be verified;

Also, the Municipal Accountant still failed to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements for the two depository accounts: the UCPB and the Philippine Postal Savings Bank, Inc. because the said banks still failed to provide them with copies of the bank statements despite the Municipal Accountant’s efforts to coordinate with them.

We also recommended that the MBO pool together unobligated balances of previously completed PPAs which may be re-programmed to a new project proposal under 20% Development Fund.

We recommended for the prompt remittance of amounts due to the concerned LGUs pursuant to Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160 to enable them to maximize its utilization for the best interest of their respective constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the optimal and judicious utilization of the GAD Fund pursuant to Section 5.b.iv of the PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01, to achieve the objective of providing equal development opportunities to men and women through gender responsive PPAs.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts.  The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.  Likewise, we recommended for the settlement of disallowances and charges within six (6) months from receipt of the Notice of Disallowance and Notice of Charge, respectively.

We recommended for the optimal utilization of the LDRRM Fund to strengthen the resilience of the constituents to disaster and climate change impacts and for the creation and filling up of the required three plantilla positions to manage and operate the local MDDRM Office in compliance with the NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-01 dated April 4, 2014.

We also recommended that the LDRRM Officer (a) attend and conduct trainings and seminars to enhance the skill set of the MDRRMO personnel pursuant to the Disaster Preparedness Minimum Standards; and (b) maximize utilization of funds intended for acquisition of DRRM equipages and prioritize the acquisition of the lacking DRRM equipment, tools and paraphernalia to comply with the minimum standards and pursuant to DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73.  This is to complement the existing equipages already available at the DRRM Office.
observations.managementAction: The utilization rate of the fund increased from 3.14% in CY 2017 to 39.85%.  Disbursements for LDRRM equipment totaled P7,122,585.00, which includes fire truck, rescue vehicle, rescue boat, among others.
observations.reason: Implementation of other priority PPAs such as the Construction of Emergency Operation Center was delayed due to limited manpower in the Engineering Office - in charge in the preparation of design/ plans.

observations.recommendation: We      recommended      that      the     (a) Municipal Budget Officer see to it that the proposed expenditures out of the SEF are verified and duly covered with appropriation before signing and forwarding the ObR to the Office of the Municipal Accountant as provided under Section 89 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I; and (b) the Bids and Awards Committee observe strictly the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods.  See to it that requisitions for procurements with same nature/scope of work with almost the same project implementation and needed at or about the same time be consolidated into a single requisition and undergo competitive bidding, except as provided in Rule XVI of the Revised IRR.
observations.managementAction: Disbursements out of the fund are in compliance with the approved budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Ecological Solid Waste Management Board prioritize the development of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 9003, oversee and ensure its effective implementation and review the plan every two years, thereafter, to ensure the protection of public health and the environment.

We also recommended that the Management consider the eventual closure of the open dumpsite and the establishment of sanitary landfill, in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003 and allocate funds therefor. observations.recommendation:

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant is advised to evaluate the need for unexpended fund balances from NGAs.  Should the funds be proven no longer needed, it should be remitted to the National Treasury as required under existing rules and regulations.  If still needed, Management should request authority from the source agency to use the money for other related projects of the Municipality.
observations.reason: Lack of available funds to liquidate/ remit the outstanding balances to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory
Committee who will conduct actual physical count of all properties of the
Municipality and submit the required RPCPPE within the prescribed timeframe
to facilitate timely verification and reconciliation of accounts.

We also recommended that the GSO maintain property cards which
should be regularly updated for proper upkeep and monitoring of properties.
The Municipal Accountant should reconcile the inventory report and the ledger
balance and adjust the affected PPE accounts for their fair presentation in the
financial statements.

We recommended that (a) ICO-Municipal Treasurer cause the immediate
submission of the 14 disbursement vouchers totaling P14,330,578.63 to the
Municipal Accountant to cause their recording in the books of accounts; and (b)
Municipal Accountant (i) draw journal entry vouchers for the recording of the
bank charges totaling P400.00 and for the adjustment of the P5,000.00 erroneous
deposit to Special Education Fund; and (ii) immediately prepare and submit the
BRS as of December 31, 2017 of the three depository accounts in order to reflect
the correct Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General
Services Officer and other requisitioning offices to prepare and submit the SSMI
and supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant for proper monitoring
of supplies and materials and recording of the expenses in the books of accounts
for the fair presentation of the account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer (a) deposit
collections to the AGDB intact and regularly as prescribed under Section 134 of
GAAM, Volume I, to avoid the risk of loss or misapplication of government
funds; (b) observe proper recording in the cashbook for proper monitoring of
collections and deposits; (c) foot and close and certify the cashbook balance at
the end of each month; (d) reconcile cashbook balance with the cash on hand
daily; and (e) reconcile the cashbook balance with accounting records quarterly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned
accountable officers to liquidate/settle their accountabilities immediately,
otherwise initiate the filing of appropriate charges against them as provided for
under COA Circular No. 2012-004. We also recommended for the suspension of
their salaries pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-004. Moreover, the Municipal
Accountant should strictly monitor the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash
advances to ensure prompt liquidation and forestall accumulation of unsettled
balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to make sure that funds collected
by the Municipality in behalf of other government agencies are remitted to them
promptly and regularly to avoid penalties and interest that may be imposed on
late remittances and prevent inconveniences to members who want to avail the
benefits being extended by these agencies.

We recommended for the prompt remittance of amounts due to the
concerned LGUs pursuant to Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July
3, 1992 to enable them to use the amounts for the best interest of their respective
constituents. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that
the account Due to LGUs are supported with complete subsidiary ledger and to
record the utilization of funds amounting to P70,992.50 for the fair presentation
of the account balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management (a) ensure that all the job order
contracts/contracts of service entered into by the Municipality clearly indicate
the specific work or task to be assigned to each employee to facilitate comparison
with the actual output; (b) stop the hiring of additional employees through job
order/contract of service if their functions are mere duplications of duties and
responsibilities of regular employees to prevent work redundancy and
unnecessary utilization of government funds; (c) assess the accomplishments of
employees hired as job order/contract of service and evaluate the necessity of
hiring them; (d) instruct the Human Resource Management Officer to
strengthen the internal control system in hiring of additional workforce through
job orders/contract of service; and (e) as a control measure, require all job order
and contract of service employees to enroll in biometric attendance system to
ensure employees’ attendance are accurately logged.

We recommended that the Municipality comply with the provisions of
Section 22 of the Omnibus Rules on Leave in granting monetization of vacation
leave credits to municipal officials and employees. We also recommended that
the Municipality ensure that the application of sick leave credits in the
monetization of the accumulated leave credits be allowed only for valid and
justifiable reasons as provided under Section 23 of the Omnibus Rules on Leave.
Moreover, require the necessary documentation before approval and payment
thereof.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all
claims/disbursements out of public funds are authorized by competent authority
and are duly supported with documents before processing for payment to
establish the validity, propriety and regularity of the claims pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) review the
regularity and completeness of supporting documents before funds are
transferred to the IAs as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (b)
record fund transfers in the books of accounts as receivable from the concerned
barangays subject to liquidation to ensure that funds are used only for the
intended purpose.

We recommended that the Management, through the: (a) Bids and
Awards Committee (i) strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its
Revised IRR; (ii) see that legal, technical and financial documents are completely
submitted by the bidders; (iii) properly conduct post-qualification to ensure the
regularity and propriety of award of contract; (b) Municipal Accountant check
the regularity and completeness of supporting documents required under
Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to facilitate review of contract,
determine compliance with RA No. 9184 and to establish the legality, validity
and propriety of the disbursements; (c) Municipal Engineer strictly monitor the
implementation of the projects and any variation/change orders and require the
contractors to submit as-built plans with date and see to it that billboards
containing relevant information regarding the project are posted within the
construction premises; and (d) ICO-Municipal Treasurer require written
authorization if the claimant of check payment is other than the payee or his
authorized representative.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, and all the municipal
officials concerned, ensure that the projects undertaken by the Municipality are
implemented and completed on time so that the intended beneficiaries could
enjoy the benefits at the earliest possible time.

We recommended that the Management, through the (a) BAC, (i) strictly
comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR regarding the
procurement of goods; and (ii) see to it that procurement are made through
public bidding to obtain the most advantageous cost to the Municipality.
Alternative methods of procurement shall be resorted to only in the highly
exceptional cases provided in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; (b) the Municipal
Accountant (i) check the regularity and completeness of supporting documents
required under Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to facilitate review of
contract, determine compliance with RA No. 9184 and to establish the legality,
validity and propriety of the disbursements; and (ii) strictly monitor the grant,
utilization and liquidation of cash advances to ensure prompt liquidation and
timely recording of related expenses; (c) require the requesting officer of
medicines to submit certification that the requested drug products fall within
and conform with the PNDF, Volume I; and (c) ICO-Municipal Treasurer make
payment by check in accordance with Section 2.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended for the optimal utilization of the LDRRM Fund to
strengthen the resilience of the constituents to disaster and climate change
impacts and for the creation and filling up of the required three plantilla
positions to manage and operate the local MDDRM Office in compliance with
the NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-01
dated April 4, 2014.

We also recommended that the LDRRM Officer (a) attend and conduct
trainings and seminars to enhance the skill set of the MDRRMO personnel
pursuant to the Disaster Preparedness Minimum Standards; and (b) maximize
utilization of funds intended for acquisition of DRRM equipages and prioritize
the acquisition of the lacking DRRM equipment, tools and paraphernalia to
comply with the minimum standards and pursuant to DILG Memorandum
Circular No. 2012-73. This is to complement the existing equipages already
available at the DRRM Office.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Budget Officer see to it that the
proposed expenditures out of the SEF are verified and duly covered with
appropriation before signing and forwarding the ObR to the Office of the
Municipal Accountant as provided under Section 89 of the NGAS Manual,
Volume I; and (b) the Bids and Awards Committee observe strictly the
requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods.
See to it that requisitions for procurements with same nature/scope of work with
almost the same project implementation and needed at or about the same time
be consolidated into a single requisition and undergo competitive bidding, except
as provided in Rule XVI of the Revised IRR.

We recommended that the Municipal Ecological Solid Waste
Management Board prioritize the development of the Municipal Solid Waste
Management Plan pursuant to the provisions of RA No. 9003, oversee and
ensure its effective implementation and review the plan every two years,
thereafter, to ensure the protection of public health and the environment.

We also recommended that the Management consider the eventual
closure of the open dumpsite and the establishment of sanitary landfill, in
compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9003 and allocate funds therefor. We
further recommended that the Management promote among its constituents
waste segregation at source, recycling and re-use of resources and consider
recommending to the Sangguniang Bayan to legislate no plastic policy in order
to reduce the volume of waste for collection and disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor reconstitute the Inventory Committee who will conduct the physical count of all PPE, submit a complete and accurate report thereon, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records.  The General Services Officer was likewise advised to maintain complete and updated perpetual inventory records which should always reconcile with the PPE general ledger balances.  Any discrepancy between these records should be properly examined to facilitate adjustment of the affected PPE account balances for their fair presentation in the financial statements.

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant should strictly monitor the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to ensure prompt liquidation and forestall accumulation of unsettled balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer the immediate submission of the lacking disbursement vouchers to the Municipal Accountant to cause their recording in the books of accounts.  We also recommended the immediate preparation and submission of the BRS as of December 31, 2016 of the three accounts in order to reflect the correct Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer and other requisitioning offices to prepare and submit the liquidation reports and documents to the Municipal Accountant for proper monitoring of supplies and materials and recording of the expenses in the books of accounts for the fair presentation of the account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) maintain subsidiary ledgers whenever necessary pursuant to Section 111 of PD No. 1445; (b) conduct proper analysis and identification of the reconciling items between the controlling accounts and the SLs; and (c) make the necessary adjustments to correct the Accounts Payable balance for its fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management cause the immediate submission of the required CY 2016 financial reports and documents.  Consider the withholding of payment of salaries of concerned officials and employees until they have complied with the requirements of the Commission.  Ensure that succeeding reports and documents are submitted on time pursuant to existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to regularly reconcile their records to facilitate the adjustment and fair presentation of Cash Local Treasury Account in the financial statements.  We also recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer deposit collections intact and regularly as prescribed under Section 134 of GAAM, Volume I, to avoid the risk of loss or misapplication of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to make sure that funds collected by the Municipality in behalf of other government agencies are remitted to them promptly and regularly to avoid penalties and interest that may be imposed on late remittances and prevent inconveniences to members who want to avail the benefits being extended by these agencies.

We recommended for the prompt remittance of amounts due to the concerned LGUs in accordance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations to enable them to maximize its utilization for the best interest of their respective constituents.

We recommended that the Management make representation with the concerned banks on the payment of loan amortizations.  If both parties agree to settle amicably, make good in the payment of its loan obligations and ensure that sufficient funds are appropriated for the payment of loan amortizations and related interests.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all payments to job orders, casuals and contractual employees are properly supported with documents pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001.  We also recommended for the issuance of contracts of service and/or job order contracts indicating the terms such as the daily wage rate, duration of work and work assignments/job description of each job order, casual and contractual employee to facilitate comparison with the actual accomplishment reports.
Also, the number of job order, casual, contractual employees should be kept reasonable, hence the need for the Human Resource Officer to assess the actual workload and manpower requirement of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims/ disbursements out of public funds are authorized by competent authority and are duly supported with documents before processing for payment to establish the validity, propriety and regularity of the claims pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the BAC strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods and civil works and to submit the complete documentation prescribed under Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
We recommended that the Management, specifically the (a) Bids and Awards Committee, strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; and (b) the Municipal Accountant ensure that claims are duly supported with complete documentation prescribed under Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish legality, validity and propriety of disbursements.  We also recommended for the submission of copy of contract/ PO and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor within five days after its perfection, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended for the optimal utilization of the LDRRMF to strengthen the resilience of the constituents to disaster and climate change impacts.  We also recommended for the transfer of the unexpended LDRRM Fund to Special Trust Fund to be utilized solely for supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities pursuant to RA No. 10121 and its IRR.  Further, we recommended for the immediate submission of disbursement voucher under check no. 152472 for proper evaluation and audit.

We recommended that Management:
Require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to prepare a certified list of taxpayers showing the names and the amounts due and collectibles based on the Real Property Tax Account Register (RPTAR)/ Taxpayer’s Index Card to provide a reliable basis in setting up of deferred and receivable accounts of SET as prescribed in Section 20 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System to avoid the incurrence of negative balances;
Require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the books of the Municipality to correct the negative balances of the affected accounts; and
Include only priority items set forth by RA No. 7160 in the preparation of the Municipal School Board Budget.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, promote among its constituents waste segregation at source and recycling and re-use of resources in order to reduce the volume of waste for collection and disposal.  We also recommended that the Management provide the solid waste collectors with the necessary clothing for their protection from potential health hazards of handling solid wastes.  Require the Municipal Health Officer to conduct necessary training to the SWM personnel to ensure that solid wastes are handled properly and to minimize health/safety risks.

We recommended that the Management secure insurance coverage for all its insurable properties with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS as a means of safeguarding physical assets and to compensate/indemnify the Municipality for any damage or loss of property.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer exercise prudence in handling Municipal funds pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445 and to consider availability of funds before incurring obligations to maintain its ability to meet current obligations.  We likewise recommended that trust funds be used solely for its intended purpose pursuant to Sections 4(3) of PD No. 1445 and 305(e) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended for the prompt implementation of priority projects funded out of grants from various government agencies.  We also recommended that the financial assistance from source agencies and collections in behalf of concerned agencies be recorded in the Trust Fund books pursuant to Section 309 of the Local Government Code.
Moreover, the Municipal Accountant is advised to evaluate the need for unexpended fund balances from NGAs.  Should the funds be proven no longer needed, it should be remitted to the National Treasury as required under existing rules and regulations.  If still needed, Management should request authority from the source agency to use the money for other related projects of the Municipality.
We recommended that Management remit the unutilized/remaining balance of PDAF totaling P15,742,869.43 to the Bureau of Treasury in strict compliance with the Supreme Court ruling.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person prepare and submit the GAD plan and budget to the PPDO to ensure its alignment with the priorities of the Province and for subsequent submission to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval in compliance with PCW/DILG/DBM/NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01.
We also recommended that the Municipal GAD Focal Point oversee the proper identification of GAD objectives and the immediate mainstreaming of GAD programs, projects and activities that aim to address gender issues and concerns in the Municipality in order that annual appropriation for GAD are properly utilized.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor with the help of the Municipal Accountant, exert efforts to enforce the settlement of suspensions and disallowances by sending letters to the concerned persons responsible/liable requiring them to settle their accountabilities.

We recommended that the Management cause the immediate submission of the
required CY 2016 financial reports and documents. Consider the withholding of
payment of salaries of concerned officials and employees until they shall have
complied with the requirements of the Commission. Ensure that succeeding
reports and documents are submitted on time pursuant to existing rules and
regulations.

We recommended that the Management promote among its constituents waste
segregation at source and recycling and re-use of resources in order to reduce
the volume of wastes for collection and disposal. We also recommended that the
Management provide the solid waste collectors with the necessary clothing for
their protection from potential health hazards of handling solid wastes. Require
the Municipal Health Officer to conduct necessary training to the SWM
personnel to ensure that solid wastes are handled properly to minimize health/
safety risks.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from
receipt of the Notice of Suspension pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and
Regulations on Settlement of Accounts. The head of the agency is responsible
for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are
complied with.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the settlement of audit
disallowances by persons liable within six months from receipt of Notice of
Disallowance in compliance with Section 10.4 of the RRSA.

We recommended that Management reconstitute the Inventory Committee who will conduct the physical count of all PPE, submit a complete and accurate report thereon, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records.  The General Services Officer was likewise advised to maintain complete and updated perpetual inventory records which should always reconcile with the PPE general ledger balances.  Any discrepancy between these records should be properly examined to facilitate adjustment of the affected PPE account balances for their fair presentation in the financial statements.

We also recommended that the amount of P2,272,843.81 be recorded in the General Fund books for the fair presentation of the Hospitals and Health Centers account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The amount of P2,272,843.81 is recorded in the Hospitals and Health Centers account in January 2016 as per JEV No. 411-16-01-03A.
observations.reason: The GSO could not proceed with the conduct of the physical inventory, as if he was facing a blank wall, because a lot of questions were raised as to whereabouts of the PPE, their accountability since no proper turn-over was made during the change of administration.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to cause the proper identification of book reconciling items for their appropriate recording in the books of accounts.  We also recommended the cancellation of stale checks and the recording of the corresponding liability accounts to reflect the correct Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to prepare and submit the liquidation documents to the Municipal Accountant for proper monitoring of fuel consumption and recording of the expenses for the fair presentation of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management cause the immediate submission of the required financial reports and documents. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer prepare and submit the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts and the same be included as an integral part of the general purpose financial statements of the Municipality, pursuant to existing rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the transfer of depository accounts with the PNB to LBP, which is the only accessible authorized government depository bank within the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Assistant Municipal Treasurer to make sure that funds collected by the Municipality in behalf of other government agencies are remitted to them promptly and regularly to avoid penalties and interest that may be imposed on late remittances and prevent inconveniences to members who want to avail the benefits being extended by these agencies.
observations.managementAction: Current accounts were remitted on time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended for the prompt remittance of amounts due to the concerned LGUs in accordance with pertinent laws, rules and regulations to enable them to maximize its utilization for the best interest of their respective constituents.

We also recommended that Management take appropriate measures to strengthen the existing collection system and improve its revenue performance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR. Procurements involving amounts more than the P100,000.00 threshold should be done through competitive bidding to promote fair competition, economy, efficiency and transparency in the procurement process and to obtain the most advantageous cost for the Municipality.  Alternative methods of procurement shall be resorted to only in the exceptional cases provided in the IRR of RA No. 9184.  

We also recommended for strict observance of the P15,000.00 limit on payments out of cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR including Appendix 11 and to submit the complete documentation prescribed under Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for further evaluation and audit.
observations.managementAction: The BAC did their best in complying with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.
observations.reason: Some documents were misplaced/lost during the processing of payments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management ensure that procurements of goods and services are made strictly in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.  Procurement planning should be done meticulously to avoid payments of goods on reimbursement basis.
observations.managementAction: The purchases out of reimbursements were brought to a minimum level especially during the second half of the calendar year under the new administration.
Collection efficiency was improved to 61.81% during the year, thus, registered a net income for SEF during the year.
observations.reason: No justification was submitted by Management on the inclusion of non-priority expenditure item in the Local School Board Budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Board hasten the development of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan, oversee and ensure its effective implementation and review the plan every two years, thereafter, to ensure the protection of public health and the environment.
observations.managementAction: The preparation of SWM Plan is on-going.
observations.reason: Still subject to approval of the Sanggunian and the Environmental Management Bureau.
